prompt.prompt_text:
You are analyzing a television commercial for corpus-linguistic and multimodal discourse analysis.

You are given sampled frames from a video clip. The frames are in chronological order.
Treat the frames as a temporal sequence from one commercial, not as independent images.

Describe the visual content of the commercial as it unfolds over time.
Describe only what is visually supported by the frames.

Do not guess dialogue, slogans, music, voice-over, or other audio content.
If something is unclear or not visible in the sampled frames, say so.

output.response_text:
The commercial begins with a young blonde girl in a lavender/purple skating dress on an indoor ice rink. She is shown in a medium shot with her arms extended as if practicing a skating movement. The background appears to be rink boards and dark windows or panels.

The next sampled frame shows a wider view of the same rink. The young girl is on skates, holding hands with a taller adult skater or coach. The girl has one leg lifted behind her in a skating pose. Over this skating scene, semi-transparent graphic text and large numbers are superimposed; the text is partly legible and includes “U.S. RDA” and “PER 2 … SERVING,” though the full wording is unclear.

The commercial then dissolves into a layered image: a close-up of an adult woman and the young girl, with the ice-rink/skating imagery still faintly visible over them. The woman is wearing a blue jacket over a light turtleneck and looks toward the camera. The girl, beside her, is holding a white item close to her mouth, possibly eating or drinking, though the exact object is not fully clear from the frame.

Near the end, the ad changes to a product display. A wrapped loaf of bread labeled “WHITE WONDER” and “WONDER” is shown on a neutral background. Above and beside it, large white text reads: “GOOD NUTRITION DOESN’T HAVE TO BE WHOLE WHEAT.” A small inset image in the upper left shows the woman and child together.

The final sampled frame is black, indicating the end or a fade-out.
